title: Unit 19: Questions, question tags, indirect questions

grammar.usage: Used to ask for information. Formed by inverting the subject and auxiliary verb, or using do/does/did for main verbs.
grammar.example: Was I on time? / Did Julie give you her email address yesterday?

grammar.usage: Used to ask for agreement or check information. Formed with an auxiliary verb/modal and a personal pronoun.
grammar.example: She is beautiful, isn't she? / Nobody knows about this, do they?

grammar.usage: Used to ask for information in a polite way. Formed with a polite phrase followed by a clause in statement word order.
grammar.example: Could you tell me what time it is? / I wonder if you have read this book.

rows: Be as main verb

Am / Is / Are / Was / Were + S (+ O)?
EX: Was I on time?
Be as auxiliary verb

Am / Is / Are / Was / Were + S + Ving (+ O)?
EX: Are Jack and Tom coming to the party tonight?
Have as auxiliary verb

Have / Has / Had + S + V3/ed (+ O)?
EX: Had Tom finished homework before Maths class yesterday?
have / mean / other main verbs

Do / Does / Did + S + Vbare (+ O)?
EX: Did Julie give you her email address yesterday?
Modals (Can, could, should, might...)

Modal + S + Vbare (+ O)?
EX: Can you give me a hand letter?
EX: Should I tell Jenny about this story?
Who

Ex1: Who is going to help you?
Ex2: Who did you see?
What

Ex1: What's the first step?
Ex2: What did you lend to Tom yesterday?
Whose
Ex: Whose responsibility is it to look after the environment?
Whom
Ex: To whom did speak?
Which
Ex: Which flavour of ice-cream is your favourite?
Where
Ex: Where did you go for your honeymoon last month?
When
Ex: When should we start?
Why
Ex: Why can't the government do something about the situation?
How
Ex: How do you spell your name?

bullets: With "who" and "what", we do not use an auxiliary verb (do/does/did) if the question word is the subject of the sentence. We use an auxiliary verb if the question word is the object of the sentence.
Used to ask someone to agree with us (falling intonation) or to check if something is true (rising intonation).

blocks.content: 2. FORMULAS & SPECIAL CASES BY VERB TYPE

rows: Be as main verb

Ex1: I am 1st member, aren't I? (Note: do not use 'am not I')
Ex2: I am not making much sense now, am I?
Ex3: She is beautiful, isn't she?
Auxiliary verbs

Ex1: You haven't lost my CD, have you?
Ex2: We are having the lesson early tomorrow, aren't we?
Modals

Ex1: People should recycle things, shouldn't they?
Ex2: There will be lots of people there, won't there?
Have as main verb
Ex1: Tom has a lovely voice, hasn't / doesn't he?
Other main verbs

Ex1: Frank lives in Germany now, doesn't he?
Ex2: Your friends enjoyed themselves at the party, didn't they?
Let's

Ex1: Let's get a DVD tonight, shall we?
Ex2: Let's go because it's getting late, isn't it?
Imperatives / Commands

Ex1: Pass me that book, will / would / could you?
Ex2: If you borrow my book, don't get it dirty, will you?
There is/are

Ex1: There's no point calling Tom now, is there?
Ex2: There's not really much point waiting, is there?
Everyone / no one / someone

→ Use a plural question tag (pronoun 'they')
Ex1: Everyone's going to be there, aren't they?
Ex2: No one wants to come, do they?
Ex3: Someone's been in here, haven't they?
Negative words (no, little, no one, never, nobody, hardly, seldom, scarcely, barely, none, neither)

→ The sentence is considered negative, so the question tag is positive.
Ex1: Nobody knows about this, do they?
have got

US: Ex: They've got a lot of money, don't they?
UK: Ex: They've got a lot of money, haven't they?

bullets: Used to ask for information or make requests in a polite way.

rows: Can / Could you tell me...?
Ex: Could you tell me what time it is?
Could you let me know...?
Ex: Could you let me know when it starts?
Do you know...?
Ex: Do you know who that woman is?
I wonder if you could tell me...
Ex: I wonder if you could tell me how much this costs.
I wonder if you know...
Ex: I wonder if you know what the starting salary is.
I would like to know...
Ex: I would like to know what your company is going to do about it.

bullets: Do not use question word order (verb before subject) after the polite phrase.
If the indirect question starts with a question word (who, what, where, etc.) and the polite introduction is a question, we still put a question mark at the end.

For Yes/No questions, we use 'if' or 'whether' to connect the polite phrase with the clause.
EX: I wonder if / whether you have read this book.

vocabulary.meaning: disagreement, typically when prolonged, public, and heated
vocabulary.example: The design of the new building has caused a lot of controversy.

vocabulary.meaning: express an objection to what someone has said or done
vocabulary.example: Students protested against the increase in tuition fees.

vocabulary.meaning: a presentation of data or information on a particular topic in a format emphasizing brevity
vocabulary.example: The company issued a factsheet about its new environmental policies.

vocabulary.meaning: a public meeting or march protesting against something or expressing views, or the action of showing how something works
vocabulary.example: They took part in a peaceful demonstration outside the city hall.

vocabulary.meaning: the main office or center of operations of an organization
vocabulary.example: The company's headquarter is located in London.

vocabulary.meaning: feeling miserable, dejected, or suffering from clinical depression
vocabulary.example: She felt depressed after receiving the disappointing exam results.

vocabulary.meaning: occurring continuously over a period of time
a person from whom one is descended, typically more remote than a grandparent
vocabulary.example: Her ancestors came from Ireland in the nineteenth century.

vocabulary.meaning: the edge of a piece of cloth or clothing which has been turned under and sewn
vocabulary.example: She needs to shorten the hem of her dress.

vocabulary.meaning: an arch of colors visible in the sky, caused by the refraction and dispersion of the sun's light in water droplets
vocabulary.example: A beautiful rainbow appeared after the rain stopped.

vocabulary.meaning: an animal, as distinct from a human being, or a fictional being
vocabulary.example: The deep ocean is home to many strange creatures.
